Nightreign is extremely frustrating and badly designed, especially for solo players. The bosses themselves are learnable, but the game punishes you for every mistake:

Total resets: Die once and you start from Night 1, Level 1, with 0 items, 0 revives, and basic flasks. No sense of progression or reward.

Timer pressure: You’re forced to rush for runes, healing, and upgrades, making it impossible to calmly learn bosses.

Multi-enemy bosses: Almost all bosses summon adds or become duos, punishing solo players.

Broken stagger system: Staggered bosses often block critical hits with their geometry, so melee players can’t finish combos.

Bad hitboxes and camera issues: Especially on dragons and huge enemies — lock-on often fails.

Nightlord AoEs: Massive arena-wide attacks that are hard to read and almost impossible to dodge.

Resource starvation: You constantly need flasks, revives, and stones, and mistakes leave you nearly helpless.

RNG dependence: Progress depends heavily on luck — right bosses, good drops, good runs.


The game looks amazing — the bosses and arenas are visually stunning — but the punishment system completely destroys the enjoyment. Even after defeating a Nightlord, you start again from zero. The design actively punishes mastery instead of rewarding it, making it one of the worst FromSoftware-inspired games I’ve ever played.

Nightreign is not a FromSoftware game.

It acts like one, looks inspired by one, but it is not made by the Elden Ring / Dark Souls / Bloodborne developers.
That is why it feels so badly balanced and unfair.
We don’t want more Nightreign FromSlop

Suggestions to fix the game:

Save progression after each night so you don’t have to restart from zero.

Bosses should be less tanky and attacks more predictable, so learning is rewarded.

Remove or adjust the timer, as it adds stress without meaningful challenge.


The game looks amazing — the bosses and arenas are visually stunning — but the punishment system destroys enjoyment. It punishes mastery instead of rewarding it, and that’s why it’s one of the worst FromSoftware-inspired games I’ve played.

Nightreign is extremely frustrating and badly designed, especially for solo players. The bosses themselves are learnable, but the game punishes you for every mistake:

Total resets: Die once and you start from Night 1, Level 1, with 0 items, 0 revives, and basic flasks. No sense of progression or reward.

Timer pressure: You’re forced to rush for runes, healing, and upgrades, making it impossible to calmly learn bosses.

Multi-enemy bosses: Almost all bosses summon adds or become duos, punishing solo players.

Broken stagger system: Staggered bosses often block critical hits with their geometry, so melee players can’t finish combos.

Bad hitboxes and camera issues: Especially on dragons and huge enemies — lock-on often fails.

Nightlord AoEs: Massive arena-wide attacks that are hard to read and almost impossible to dodge.

Resource starvation: You constantly need flasks, revives, and stones, and mistakes leave you nearly helpless.

RNG dependence: Progress depends heavily on luck — right bosses, good drops, good runs.


The game looks amazing — the bosses and arenas are visually stunning — but the punishment system completely destroys the enjoyment. Even after defeating a Nightlord, you start again from zero. The design actively punishes mastery instead of rewarding it, making it one of the worst FromSoftware-inspired games I’ve ever played.

I think I've figured it out—I've figured out why most, if not all, of the stages in Tekken 8 look flat. The thing is, in past Tekken games, particularly Tekken 4 (my favorite in terms of stage variety, presentation, and inspiration) and Tekken 6, the stages had depth to them. Yes, there was clear and obvious visual presentation of the foreground and the background, making the stages feel alive and like actual 3D spaces.

In Tekken 8, almost every stage looks flat. Like everything looks so colorful and sharp that you can't tell the difference between the background and the foreground most of the time. Tekken 8 stages, while varied, lack personality for some reason, too. Also, why do Namco devs love to hurt our eyes with too-red stages? Let me preface by saying that I love it so far, but I do have feedback, only for improvements sake. I'm not trying to be a hater. I am 100% buying this game. I'm really just trying to offer ideas to spitball in the developer round table discussions.

 Yes, the sword placement is a bit odd.

 I also wouldn't mind a bit of slow motion as an option to turn on and off with the execution kills.

 The hand having the scars or tattoo on it is a bit obvious. Unless it's something other people in the world have, I'd tone it down a bit. Maybe it should only be that bright or even glow slightly as an indicator for usage of powers, like when he could use magic to talk to the corpse, which was really cool. Make it work like how the medallion in the Witcher did.

The range at which you actually struck enemies was a bit close. Maybe incorporate just a bit more magnetism into the swinging so there is less flailing randomly when looking directly at an opponent. 

It might already be a thing, but incorporating the bat teleport into combat would be cool to dodge or even get behind opponents for a backstabbing bonus, like draining more of the opponents stamina or just more damage. 

I found it odd that no npcs reacted to the main character when he started doing vampire stuff. Like why aren't they shocked when he starts walking up walls beside them, or verbally declare "he's a f***ing vampire" when you bite them in combat? I know vampires are known in this world at this point, but I would be a bit caught off guard by these things while chilling or in the middle of a fight. Maybe even a fear mechanic where if you are savage enough with your powers, some humans flee during combat?

Are there no consumable items for flights? Like fire/wooden stakes for vampires, throwing daggers, or possibly even late 13th century gunpowder bombs? This could totally be a moot point because it would be hard to balance, but just a thought, if you wanted to add it. 

Coen seemed very quiet during combat. No real grunts or saying anything from him or his enemies. Overall, combat was a bit quiet overall. The music seemed a bit tame. I know this would be a lot to add to because it requires recording more tracks, but if you have the time. 

It would be very fitting here, but not necessary to add a visual effect when walking outside in the daytime from indoors. Fallout New Vegas had this mechanic after loading outside where the sun would glare for just a split second. I know Coen is "human" during the day, but I can't shake the feeling that he would still instinctively feel the sun a bit more when he's half creature of the night. You could even have him say something like "It's so bright out" or "suns glaring" 😉 from time to time as he walks out. Maybe even have him lift his hands to block the sun, just to give him a bit more life. It's not necessary at all, but these things are what makes games feel surreal. 

One big thing is weather. I'm sure it's incorporated to some extent, but I can just imagine how cool it would have been to go into that cathedral as rain pours down while you teleport from rooftop to rooftop. In those close up first person scenes while he's investigating things, it would be so cool to see lightning flashing outside as you hear the rain shower down on the roof. The atmosphere is perfect for it. 

A perfect movement system here would be a slow falling levitation for falling from heights would be very cool. At around 21:14, Coen jumps, then teleports while falling two separate times. The game "Control" from 2019 has an awesome slow falling levitation ability you can activate while falling that would have fit perfectly right there. It would make the falling look a bit less awkward.  I know you already have shadow step and the claw decent, so this may be a bit redundant, but I think it would just be cool.

I hope there is some form of magic "jedi mind trick powers" you can use during dialogue by leveling up. I don't wanna have to pay people for information. I want to use freaky vampire powers to get info I need. Maybe it even works on vampires at high enough of a level.

Please let there be a way for Coen to be evil. Id love to see a full bag guy ending where Coen decides to become the new leader of the vampires.

I know it's a completely different direction, but you've already hinted at other creatures coming about in this universe. Please give us a lycanthrope (werewolf) story based DLC where Coen has to stop werewolves from taking over after the vampires are defeated. You could make it a full month leading up to a full moon in the lunar cycle that the people in this time period would 100% know about in real life. It would absolutely fit perfectly into your day and night system where time passes that's a core mechanic already. Just a thought. 

Again, I'm just humbly making suggestions here. If you incorporated none of this, I'm absolutely still going to get this game. I just want to toss some ideas your way. I know I'm some nobody, but I'd fully be willing to help brainstorm more things or explain more in depth and would happily sign any NDA's I was asked to. This game is exactly the type of game I would make if I was involved in this type of work. I'm so happy to see something this cool being made by people who worked on one of my favorite games of all time. You guys are doing great, keep it up. I see passion in this project, which is something that's sorely lacking in the gaming industry these days.
By far my most anticipated game this year. Keep up the great work and take your time, Rebel Wolves. Deliver us the spiritual successor to the The Witcher 3 that we want

Seeing Digimon slowly coming back to it's roots, while keeping fresh and exciting, is not something I was expecting to happen so soon. Ever since my first contact with Digimon World 1 back then, I have been completely invested in this franchise. Be it informational booklets about new digital monsters, game titles, anime, anything and everything, I was there for it. So I'm very happy to see it's taking the right steps in order to reclaim it's crown. And when that happens, I'll surely be there as well!
Congratulations to anyone involved in this game, you all did a great job.

Best Digimon game so far. An epic story, a big roster including new entries alongside the various iconic / fan favorite Digimon as well as returnees we haven't seen in many years and notable improvements in gameplay compared to past titles. There are some small hiccups (like not being able to digivolve in the Digifarm as an example) but nothing much to outdo all the positives. The environment truly felt alive as you'd expect from the Digital World and much of the scenery quite breathtaking to watch. Not to mention having the option to ride on a Digimon is certified goals for any fan. As a Mythology enthusiast and a Greek myself who always loves seeing all the various interpretations Greco-Roman in particular can take, it has been a treat getting to know the Olympos XII and traversing throughout Illiad. Each one of them standing out and just oozing personality in their own unique way. And I can say the same for a few of the Titans as the antagonists. I feel like a great job has been done exploring and developing them as characters. And the biggest highlight for me will have to be Inori and Aegiomon. I just loved their chemistry from beginning to end. Both of them having their individual arcs that interwine together and the result was just beautiful to witness. Honestly, they are now among my favorite relationships across all Digimon media. They were truly great. In overall, an awesome game that's a step in the right direction for the franchise which deserves all the recognition, especially in the west, and you could see that desire from the effort that was put. Like, even as someone who usually prefers the Japanese voiceovers, an English dub was very pleasant to see. Kudos to all the work and looking forward to what else the future will bring.

Ace Combat 5: The Unsung War (Squadron Leader in Europe) is considered by many fans to be the best game in the series from the PS2 era. This is also the first in the series where, in addition to mini-games related to takeoff and landing at an airport/aircraft carrier, air refueling was introduced with the participation of the McDonnell Douglas KC-10A Extender - the standard air tanker of the Ace Combat series, which required the addition of the MiG-21's fuel intake capability (in reality it did not have such a capability), fighters from the MiG and Su family, and the F-5 family. A gripping story revealing the biggest secret during the conflict between Osea and Yuktobania—that it was Belka pulling the strings, exploiting, among other things, the Osean military's dislike of Vincent Harling's pacifist policies and his own ambition to avenge the Belka War of 15 years earlier. The fate of the members of the 108th Tactical Fighter Squadron "Wardog," and the fulfillment of the Razgriz prophecy make AC5 unique. The Warsaw Philharmonic Orchestra also contributed to the game's soundtrack. AC5 received a PS4 port as a pre-order bonus for AC7: Skies Unknown.

The game features 53 aircraft within a "family" of aircraft trees. Joining classics such as the F-15C, F-15, F-16C, F-5E (debuting in AC04), Su-37 and F-4E are the A-6E and Gripen C, and featuring in subsequent games are the Northrop F-20A Tigershark, Grumman X-29A, MiG-21-93 "Fishbed", Grumman EA-6B Prowler and its successor, the Boeing EA-18G Growler (first flown in 2006), Panavia PA-200 Tornado GR.4, General Dynamics F-16XL, MiG-1.44 "Flatpack", Sukhoi Su-27 "Flanker-B" and Su-34 "Fullback", McDonnell Douglas F-4G Phantom II, Dassault Mirage 2000D, Grumman F-14B Bombcat and F-14D Super. The Tomcat, the Lockheed Martin F-35C Lightning II (still based on the X-35C, the production version debuted only in AC7), the Lockheed Martin FB-22 Strike Raptor, the S-32, and the fictional North Osea Gründer Industries ADF-01 FALKEN. The game also features aircraft unique to this installment of Ace Combat, such as the F-4X, F-16E Block 60, Panavia PA-200 Tornado GR.1 and ECR, the Dassault Rafale B (with CFT tanks), and the BAE Hawk T.1, which played a key role in the game's plot. Aircraft like the Rafale B should return in Ace Combat 8.

The game's flagship fighter is the Grumman F-14A Tomcat, in Japanese, North American, and PAL versions. In the French version, this role is filled by the Dassault Rafale M, and in the Spanish version, by the Eurofighter EF-2000 Typhoon, despite the lack of a carrier-based version. In retrospect, it turns out that the Tomcat wasn't the best choice for a flagship fighter. The Super Hornet, Rafale M, and F-35C would have been better in this role.

30 years of the Ace Combat series in 2025.
